candidate_problems.text: What precise structural conditions make the 'public-good opposition/private-good conformity' mechanism sufficient to sustain a dominant power structure, as opposed to cases where norms, ideology, coercion, or explicit coordination are doing most of the explanatory work?

How can the conjecture be operationalized and tested empirically: what observable signatures would distinguish decentralized incentive-lock-in from mere apathy, preference falsification, bad faith, or active collusion, especially in hiring, promotion, citation, policing, or governance systems?

Which interventions actually change the equilibrium rather than merely expressing opposition—e.g. lowering dissent costs, making reform contributions attributable, creating selective incentives, or building coalitions—and under what feedback structures do such changes produce durable reform instead of symbolic or reversible gains?
